nodes.name: adjective
nodes.context: Adjectives tune nouns: the swift knight, a circumspect player. Precision instruments, not decoration.

nodes.name: adverb
nodes.context: Adverbs tune verbs (and adjectives): moved carefully, extremely sharp. Many wear the -ly badge; the sneaky ones (very, quite, never) do not.

nodes.name: conjunction
nodes.context: Conjunctions join: and, but, or, because, although. The hinges every compound and complex sentence swings on.

nodes.name: noun
nodes.context: Nouns name things: people, places, objects, ideas. The first sorting job — everything a sentence talks ABOUT is a noun or acts like one.

nodes.name: preposition
nodes.context: Prepositions place things in space and time: under the board, before the storm, across town. They start little location- phrases that glue onto sentences.

nodes.name: pronoun
nodes.context: Pronouns stand in for nouns: he, she, it, they. Each points back at something — a broken pointer (unclear antecedent) is the classic bug.

nodes.name: verb
nodes.context: Verbs are the engine: actions (run, build) and states (is, seems). No verb, no sentence — the one part of speech a sentence cannot live without.

nodes.name: complete
nodes.context: Complete sentence = subject + predicate that can stand alone. Fragments are missing a piece ('because the storm came'); run- ons are two sentences taped together without a hinge. Diagnosis and repair — the editor's first skill.

nodes.name: object
nodes.context: The object receives the action: the knight took THE ROOK. Ask the verb 'what?' — the answer is the object. Subject-verb- object is English's default word order; scrambling it is what other languages (and bad sentences) do.

nodes.name: predicate
nodes.context: The predicate is everything the sentence SAYS about the subject — the verb plus its cargo. From Latin prae + dicare, 'to proclaim': what is proclaimed about the subject. 'The knight | took the rook': subject, then predicate. Every complete sentence splits into these two pieces.

nodes.name: subject
nodes.context: The subject is WHO or WHAT the sentence is about — find the verb, ask who is doing it. In 'the knight took the rook', the subject is the knight.

nodes.name: complex
nodes.context: Complex sentence: an independent clause plus one or more dependent ones. Where writing stops sounding like a list and starts sounding like thinking.

nodes.name: compound
nodes.context: Compound sentence: two independent clauses hinged with a conjunction (and, but, so) or a semicolon. Two complete thoughts, one sentence, equal partners.

nodes.name: dependent
nodes.context: A dependent clause has a subject and verb but leans on the main sentence ('although he was winning, ...'). The leaning word (although, because, when) is what makes it lean.

nodes.name: independent
nodes.context: An independent clause is a complete sentence living inside a bigger one — it could stand alone if you cut it free.
